title: Integrable Stochastic Particle Systems and Macdonald Processes
speaker: Alexei Borodin
abstract: A large class of one dimensional stochastic particle systems are predicted to share the same universal long-time/large-scale behavior. By studying certain integrable models within this (Kardar-Parisi-Zhang) universality class we access what should be universal statistics and phenomena. In this talk we focus on two different integrable exclusion processes: q-TASEP and ASEP.

proof_stages.label: KPZ/TASEP benchmark and free-fermion baseline
proof_stages.summary: The talk fixes TASEP as a basic random growth/exclusion model in the KPZ universality class, recalls the KPZ scaling exponents and Johansson's Tracy-Widom fluctuation theorem, and contrasts this with the already-solved determinantal/free-fermion family of models.

proof_stages.label: Non-determinantal hierarchy and observable principle
proof_stages.summary: The central new direction is identified as a hierarchy of non-free-fermion KPZ models for which a rich enough set of observables can be averaged explicitly and can characterize the stochastic system.

proof_stages.label: q-TASEP and ASEP moment formulas
proof_stages.summary: For q-TASEP and ASEP the speaker displays special exponential/current observables whose joint moments are given by nested contour-integral formulas with the same non-determinantal interaction factor.

proof_stages.label: Moment-to-law and asymptotic extraction
proof_stages.summary: Equal-index moment formulas are summed into q-Laplace/Fredholm determinant expressions, whose degenerations to the semi-discrete Brownian polymer admit steepest-descent asymptotics yielding Tracy-Widom fluctuations and clarifying why naive lower-level moment summation is ill-posed.

proof_stages.label: Macdonald-process source of the formulas
proof_stages.summary: Macdonald processes are introduced as probability measures built from Macdonald polynomials, whose difference-operator eigenfunction structure and degeneration hierarchy explain where the solvable stochastic models and their good observables come from.

proof_stages.label: Markov dynamics and eigenvalue observable calculus
proof_stages.summary: The Macdonald formalism becomes probabilistically useful through explicit Markov operators preserving the measures and through applying diagonal Macdonald difference operators to the Cauchy identity, with the t=0 specialization producing a local triangular-array dynamics whose edge projection is q-TASEP and whose eigenvalues recover the q-TASEP observables.

proof_stages.label: Bethe-ansatz and quantum many-body verification
proof_stages.summary: As an alternative to the Macdonald-process construction, the desired moment observables satisfy closed linear evolution equations that can be solved by Bethe ansatz, degenerating to the quantum delta Bose gas moment equations for the stochastic heat equation and connecting with replica computations.

proof_stages.label: Synthesis and open hierarchy levels
proof_stages.summary: The conclusion summarizes the new analyzable 1+1-dimensional growth models, the Macdonald-process and quantum-many-body routes to explicit observables, the Fredholm/asymptotic consequences, and the remaining unexplored parts of the hierarchy.

examples.name: Traffic-flow interpretation of TASEP
examples.note: Cars moving right when there is space make the exclusion rule intuitive, but no argument later depends on the traffic model itself.
examples.engagement_reason: The traffic picture is used as a cars-on-a-road analogy for the TASEP jump rules, with no separate computation or analysis of traffic flow.

examples.name: Simulation of triangular-array q-TASEP crystallization
examples.note: Computer pictures of the rescaled triangular-array dynamics illustrate the emergence of lattice-like curves and the limit toward the semi-discrete polymer regime.
examples.engagement_reason: The triangular-array q-TASEP dynamics are explicitly defined by neighbor-dependent jump rates, related to left-edge q-TASEP, and visually reasoned through in the crystallization simulation.
